questions.prompt: A relation is said to be in First Normal Form (1NF) if and only if:

options.text: Every non-key attribute is fully functionally dependent on the primary key
Every attribute contains only atomic (indivisible) values, with no repeating groups or arrays
There are no transitive dependencies
Every determinant is a candidate key

questions.explanation: 1NF eliminates non-atomic attributes — every cell holds a single value, no repeating groups, lists, or nested records. The other options describe 2NF, 3NF and BCNF respectively.

questions.prompt: A relation R(A, B, C, D) has primary key (A, B) and the FD B → D holds. Which normal form is violated?

options.text: 1NF
2NF (because D is partially dependent on the key)
3NF only
BCNF only

questions.explanation: D depends only on B, which is part of the composite primary key (A, B). That is a partial dependency, which violates 2NF. The relation is in 1NF but must be decomposed to reach 2NF.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is the strictest condition that defines Boyce-Codd Normal Form (BCNF)?

options.text: Every non-prime attribute must be fully dependent on every candidate key
For every non-trivial functional dependency X → Y, X must be a superkey
The relation must have only one candidate key
There must be no multi-valued dependencies

questions.explanation: BCNF requires that for every non-trivial FD X → Y, X is a superkey. This is a stronger condition than 3NF (which permits FDs whose right-hand side is a prime attribute even if X is not a superkey).

questions.prompt: Fourth Normal Form (4NF) addresses anomalies caused by:

options.text: Transitive functional dependencies
Partial functional dependencies on composite keys
Non-trivial multi-valued dependencies (MVDs) where the determinant is not a superkey
Lossy join decompositions

questions.explanation: 4NF eliminates non-trivial multi-valued dependencies (MVDs) X →→ Y where X is not a superkey. 3NF and 2NF deal with FDs; lossless-join is a separate decomposition property.

questions.prompt: Decomposition of a relation R into R1 and R2 is called a lossless-join decomposition if:

options.text: R1 ∪ R2 = R
R1 × R2 = R (Cartesian product reproduces R)
(R1 ⋈ R2) yields exactly the original relation R, without spurious tuples
R1 and R2 share no attributes

questions.explanation: Lossless-join means joining R1 and R2 on their common attributes yields exactly the original relation R, with no extra (spurious) tuples and no information loss. This is guaranteed when the common attribute(s) form a superkey of at least one of R1 or R2.
